rulings.Case Title: ROY POOLE VS. CALIFORNIA PACIFIC MEDICAL FOUNDATION et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Ntc Of Mtn And Mtn To Exclude The Expert Testimony Of Leslie Zun M.D. And/Or Dennis Bietz R.N.
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, August 19, 2014, Line 4, DEFENDANT SUTTER WEST BAY HOSPITALS (DBA CALIFORNIA PACIFIC Motion To Exclude The Expert Testimony Of Leslie Zun M.D. And/Or Dennis Bietz R.N. Pro Tem Judge Steven Stein, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Motion to exclude expert trial testimony of Leslie Zun, M.D. is in all respects granted. The plaintiff failed to comply with the express terms of the previous Order dated August 4, 2014 requiring Dr. Zun to be produced for an expert witness discovery deposition on or before a date certain. The Court accepts defendant's withdrawal of that portion of the motion to exclude the expert trial testimony by Dennis Bietz, R.N. To the extent an issue remains regarding restoration of Nurse Bietz to plaintiff's Expert Witness List, it is referred for resolution to the trial Judge. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to sbs@knoxricksen.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: JOSEPH CONNELLY VS. COLLEEN HOLLAND et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Ntc Of Mtn And Mtn To Reopen Discovery (Ccp 2024.050)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, August 19, 2014, Line 1, PLAINTIFF JOSEPH CONNELLY Motion To Reopen Discovery (CCP 2024.050). Pro Tem Judge Steven Stein, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Plaintiff's motion to reopen discovery is denied. Plaintiff filed for bankruptcy on October 16, 2013, six days prior to the initial discovery cut-off date. Filing for bankruptcy by a plaintiff in a civil matter does not result in an automatic stay. (Ingersoll-Rand Financial Corp. v. Miller Min. Co. (9th Cir. 1987) 817 F.2d 1424.) Discovery remained open. Retention of new counsel by plaintiff on April 8, 2014 is a factor considered in the exercise of discretion to reopen discovery after setting a new trial date. But does not compel reopening. Failure to complete discovery by plaintiff's two earlier counsel is considered as well. (Regan vs. Lanet (1987) 197 Cal.App.3d 353). Based on the entire discovery record to date, plaintiff's burden to show good cause to reopen discovery has not been met. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to sbs@knoxricksen.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: MELVIN L. COCKHREN II VS. PACIFIC GAS AND ELECTRIC COMPANY et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on Calendar for Tuesday, August 19, 2014, Line 7, DEFENDANT PACIFIC GAS AND ELECTRIC COMPANY's MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT. Granted. The California Public Utilities Code sec. 489(a) requires every public utility to file a "tariff" - a schedule "showing all rates, tolls, rentals, charges, and classifications ... together with all rules, contracts, privileges, and facilities which in any manner affect or relate to rates, tolls, rentals, classifications, or service" - with the California Public Utilities Commission. "Such a tariff, when approved by the PUC, has the force of law." (Pacific Bell v. Public Utilities Com'n (2000) 79 Cal.App.4th 269, 274.) "A condition imposed by a tariff binds a utility's customers without regard to whether a contract is signed by the customer and without regard to the customer's actual knowledge of the tariff." (Los Angeles Cellular Telephone Co. v. Superior Court (1998) 65 Cal.App.4th 1013, 1018.) Tariff Electric Rule no. 14 provides that "PG&E specifically maintains the right to interrupt its service deliveries, without liability to the Customers¿when, in PG&E's sole opinion, such interruption is necessary for reasons including, but not limited to, the following: 1. Safety of a customer, a PG&E employee, or the public at large." The undisputed evidence establishes that PG&E's troubleman determined that the power had to be turned off for safety purposes. (UMF 6.) As such, the undisputed evidence establishes that PG&E did not breach a duty by turning off the power. In any event, assuming PG&E did breach a duty by turning off the power, Plaintiff does not raise a triable issue of material fact as to causation. The undisputed evidence establishes that Plaintiff did not leave the candle burning for light, but rather, "there wasn't a purpose" for the candle burning. (UMF 15, Neubert Decl., Ex. B, Plaintiff's Deposition Transcript, p. 149:13-28, 158:12-14; 186:1-16.) Therefore, Plaintiff cannot establish that PG&E turning off power caused Plaintiff to use a candle at the time the fire ignited. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and in compliance with Code of Civil Procedure 437c(g) and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EHG)

rulings.Case Title: CRAIG K MARTIN VS. DOES 1-50, INCLUSIVE
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO STRIKE 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on Calendar for Tuesday, August 19, 2014, Line 11, DEFENDANT MICHAEL KECK's MOTION TO STRIKE 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT. Granted. The Court must engage in a two-step process when ruling on an anti-SLAPP motion under CCP § 425.16. "First, the court decides whether the defendant has made a threshold showing that the cause of action is one arising from protected activity. . . . If the court finds such a showing has been made . . . it then determines whether the plaintiff has demonstrated a probability of prevailing on the claim." (Equilon Enterprises v. Consumer Cause Inc. (2002) 29 Cal.4th 53, 67.) In determining whether the allegations arise from a protected activity, the court's "focus is on the principal thrust or gravamen of the causes of action, i.e., the allegedly wrongful and injury producing conduct that provides the foundation for the claims." (Castleman v. Sagaser (2013) 216 Cal.App.4th 481, 490-91.) Here, each of Plaintiff's causes of action arise out of the State Bar disciplinary proceedings brought against him or the defense of the federal suit filed by Plaintiff. Thus, the first prong of the anti-SLAPP statute is met. Plaintiff has not demonstrated a probability of prevailing. A plaintiff is required to "show both that the claim is legally sufficient and there is admissible evidence that, if credited, would be sufficient to sustain a favorable judgment." (McGarry v. University of San Diego 154 Cal.App.4th 97, 108.) Plaintiff here failed to present any evidence in opposition, and therefore failed to demonstrate a probability of prevailing. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4:00 p.m. stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if in pro per on the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EHG)

rulings.Case Title: CRAIG K MARTIN VS. DOES 1-50, INCLUSIVE
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Special Motion And Special Motion To Strike First Amended Verified Complaint Conspiracy And Damages
rulings.Rulings: Matter on Calendar for Tuesday, August 19, 2014, Line 11, DEFENDANT THEODORE ELIOPOULOS' Special Motion To Strike First Amended Verified Complaint Conspiracy And Damages. Granted. The Court must engage in a two-step process when ruling on an anti-SLAPP motion under CCP § 425.16. "First, the court decides whether the defendant has made a threshold showing that the cause of action is one arising from protected activity. . . . If the court finds such a showing has been made . . . it then determines whether the plaintiff has demonstrated a probability of prevailing on the claim." (Equilon Enterprises v. Consumer Cause Inc. (2002) 29 Cal.4th 53, 67.) Here, the conduct alleged against Defendant constitutes protected activity because it was speech concerning a matter of public interest made in a public forum. Further, Plaintiff has failed to demonstrate a probability of prevailing. A plaintiff is required to "show both that the claim is legally sufficient and there is admissible evidence that, if credited, would be sufficient to sustain a favorable judgment." (McGarry v. University of San Diego 154 Cal.App.4th 97, 108.) Plaintiff here failed to present any evidence in opposition, and therefore failed to demonstrate a probability of prevailing. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4:00 p.m. stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if in pro per on the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EHG)

rulings.Case Title: CRAIG K MARTIN VS. DOES 1-50, INCLUSIVE
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on Calendar for Tuesday, August 19, 2014, Line 11, DEFENDANT ANTHONY LINCOLN's DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT. Demurrer sustained without leave to amend. The Complaint fails to allege any conduct by Defendant to support any of the causes of action asserted against him. The Complaint also fails to allege any facts to support the formation or existence of a conspiracy between Defendant and any other named Defendant. Further, Plaintiff bases Defendant's liability as a coconspirator on an erroneous legal basis-i.e., that Defendant became a member of the alleged conspiracy by filing a complaint against Plaintiff with the State Bar. Thus, the Complaint cannot be cured by amendment because such conduct could not form nor further any such alleged conspiracy. Moreover, with respect to any claims resulting from alleged violations of the Ralph Act (Civ. Code § 51.7) or the Bane Act (Civ. Code § 52.1), Plaintiff fails to allege that any violent acts or threatened violent acts were committed by anyone, let alone the moving party. Plaintiff alleges verbal abuse by another named defendant but this is not sufficient to state a constitutional deprivation of rights per Title 42 U.S.C. § 1983. (Oltarzewski v. Ruggiero (9th Cir. 1987) 830 F.2d 136, 139.) Nor are any facts alleged linking the moving party to this conduct other than the bare conclusion, without more, that they are conspirators. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4:00 p.m. stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if in pro per on the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EHG)
